skill_name: Cleft Sentences & Ellipsis (It is… / What… / Omission)

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: It was the head teacher ___ called my parents about the incident, not the class tutor.
distractor_rationale: which is used for things, not people, so it is grammatically incorrect here
that is acceptable in informal use but this item specifically tests who for a human subject in a formal cleft
whom is the object form and cannot be used when the cleft focus is the subject of the following clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: What the children ___ most after the long trip was a warm meal and a good rest.
distractor_rationale: present simple breaks the past-time narrative established by 'after the long trip'
present continuous cannot describe a completed past state of need
present perfect cannot anchor to a specific completed past situation described as finished

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: It ___ the noisy neighbours who kept us awake all night, not the traffic outside.
distractor_rationale: present simple is incorrect because the sentence describes a completed past event signalled by 'kept'
were does not agree with the singular it-cleft subject construction, which always takes a singular verb
present perfect cannot be used in a cleft sentence referring to a specific completed past event

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Never ___ such a disorganised meeting in all my years working in this company.
distractor_rationale: after negative fronting with Never, subject-verb inversion is obligatory, so the subject must follow the auxiliary
past simple with normal word order is doubly wrong: it lacks inversion and uses the wrong tense for a life-experience statement
did I attend uses past simple inversion, but the present perfect is required for a life-experience statement with no specific time reference

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: What really surprised everyone at the conference ___ the speaker's final announcement about the new project funding.
distractor_rationale: were is incorrect because the complement 'the speaker's final announcement' is a singular noun phrase, requiring a singular verb
is uses present tense, which is inconsistent with the past-time context of a completed conference event
are is doubly wrong: it is plural and present, while the sentence requires singular past tense

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: A: Did you and Sara manage to finish the report before the deadline? B: Sara did, but I ___.
distractor_rationale: repeating the full verb phrase 'didn't finish' is grammatically possible but violates the ellipsis rule being tested, which requires omission of the repeated verb phrase
don't uses present simple, which does not match the past-tense auxiliary 'did' established in the question
wasn't uses a be-auxiliary, which cannot substitute for the do-auxiliary required by the main verb 'finish'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: It was the faulty cable ___ caused the entire system to shut down during the presentation.
distractor_rationale: who is reserved for people; a cable is a thing, so who is grammatically incorrect here
which cannot introduce the subordinate clause in an it-cleft construction; only that or who are used after the focused element
what introduces a what-cleft and cannot follow the focused element inside an it-cleft structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Not only ___ the deadline, but the team also delivered results that exceeded the client's expectations.
distractor_rationale: they met uses normal declarative word order, which is obligatorily inverted after negative fronting with Not only
they did meet places the subject before the auxiliary, violating the subject-verb inversion required after Not only
have they met uses present perfect inversion, but the past-time context established by 'delivered' and the completed project requires past simple

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: What the manager ___ is a clearer breakdown of the monthly costs, not a general summary.
distractor_rationale: want without the third-person singular -s ending fails subject-verb agreement with the singular subject 'the manager'
are wanting uses a plural auxiliary and the progressive aspect, both of which are incorrect: the subject is singular and 'want' is a stative verb not used in continuous form
is wanting incorrectly uses the progressive aspect; 'want' is a stative verb that does not take continuous forms in standard English

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Only after the investigation was completed ___ the full extent of the damage to the building.
distractor_rationale: the engineers discovered uses normal declarative word order, which must be inverted after the fronted negative adverbial 'Only after'
the engineers did discover places the subject before the auxiliary, violating the obligatory inversion after 'Only after'
had the engineers discovered uses past perfect inversion, but the main clause describes a single completed past action after a completed condition, requiring past simple with do-inversion

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: It was the manager ___ approved the new budget, not the assistant.
distractor_rationale: which is used for things, not people in a cleft sentence
that is used for things or in restrictive relative clauses but not the standard choice for a person in an it-cleft when who is available and being tested
whom is an object pronoun and cannot function as the subject of 'approved' in this cleft

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: What Sara really ___ is a long holiday after months of working overtime.
distractor_rationale: bare infinitive 'need' lacks the third-person singular -s required for the subject 'Sara' in present tense
the progressive form cannot be used with stative verbs like 'need' in this context
past tense 'needed' contradicts the present-tense context of 'after months of working overtime' implying the need is current

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Never ___ so many people waiting outside the clinic when I arrived at eight in the morning.
distractor_rationale: placing the subject before the auxiliary violates the obligatory subject-verb inversion after negative fronting
present perfect 'have I seen' is impossible because the sentence describes a specific completed past moment ('when I arrived'), requiring past perfect
present perfect without past reference cannot describe a situation observed at a specific past time expressed by 'when I arrived'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: It ___ the heavy rain that caused the roof to leak, not the old pipes.
distractor_rationale: present tense 'is' is wrong because the cleft focuses on a past event; 'caused' in the relative clause confirms the past time frame
'has been' introduces present perfect aspect, which is incompatible with the simple past 'caused' in the following clause
'were' requires a plural subject, but 'the heavy rain' is a singular uncountable noun

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: What surprised the whole team most ___ the speed at which the new system processed all the data.
distractor_rationale: 'were' is wrong because the complement 'the speed' is a singular noun phrase, so the linking verb must be singular
present tense 'is' does not match the past-tense context established by 'surprised', indicating the reaction occurred at a specific past moment
'has been' shifts the clause into present perfect aspect, which conflicts with the simple past 'surprised' describing a completed past reaction

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: It was the manager ___ approved the new schedule, not the assistant.
distractor_rationale: which is used for things, not people — a classic learner error in it-clefts
that he adds a redundant subject pronoun, making the clause grammatically broken
whom is an object pronoun and cannot function as the subject of the relative clause here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: What Anna really ___ is more time to finish the project before the deadline.
distractor_rationale: need without -s fails subject-verb agreement — the subject 'Anna' is third-person singular
are needing incorrectly uses progressive aspect with a stative verb in a what-cleft
have needed uses present perfect, which contradicts the present-tense context of the what-cleft

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: A: Did you call the supplier this morning? B: No, but Sara ___.
distractor_rationale: called repeats the full verb, which is non-standard ellipsis — the auxiliary alone is the correct short form
has introduces present perfect and fails to match the past simple auxiliary in the question
was introduces a copula that does not correspond to the action verb 'call' in the question

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: It was the faulty cable ___ caused the entire system to shut down unexpectedly last night.
distractor_rationale: who is reserved for people in it-clefts; 'cable' is a thing
which it adds a redundant pronoun subject, creating a double-subject error
what cannot introduce the relative clause in an it-cleft — it belongs in what-cleft constructions

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: What surprised everyone at the meeting ___ the director's sudden decision to cancel the project.
distractor_rationale: were uses plural agreement, but the complement 'the director's sudden decision' is singular
is being incorrectly uses present progressive, which is unnatural with a stative linking verb in this past context
has been uses present perfect, which is inconsistent with the past-tense narrative context of the sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Never ___ such a well-organised conference in all my years of working in this industry.
distractor_rationale: I have attended fails to apply subject-auxiliary inversion after the negative fronted adverb 'Never'
I attended uses past simple without inversion, breaking both the tense and the word-order rule for negative fronting
did I attend uses past simple inversion, but 'Never have I' requires present perfect to express a life-experience meaning

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: It is the quality of the ingredients ___ makes this dish so special, not the cooking technique.
distractor_rationale: which can introduce relative clauses but cannot be used in an it-cleft focusing on a noun phrase subject
who is for people; 'the quality of the ingredients' is not a person
what introduces what-clefts and cannot follow the it-cleft structure 'It is … that'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Not only ___ the report late, but he also forgot to include the financial summary.
distractor_rationale: he submitted places the subject before the auxiliary, violating the inversion rule required after 'Not only'
he did submit uses emphatic do without inversion, which is still incorrect after a fronted negative adverbial
had he submitted uses past perfect inversion, which is inconsistent with the simple past context of the sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: What the team had worked so hard to achieve ___ a fair and transparent evaluation process for all staff.
distractor_rationale: were applies plural agreement, but the complement 'a fair and transparent evaluation process' is a singular noun phrase
had been uses past perfect, which creates an illogical double past-perfect structure after 'had worked'
is shifts to present tense, which contradicts the past-tense context established by 'had worked'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: A: Has anyone reviewed the updated contract yet? B: James has, but the legal team ___.
distractor_rationale: didn't uses past simple auxiliary, which fails to match the present perfect auxiliary 'has' established in the question
hasn't been introduces a passive or copular structure that does not correspond to the active verb 'reviewed' being elided
don't uses present simple auxiliary, which is inconsistent with the present perfect tense signalled by 'has' in the question

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: It was the manager which approved the new schedule last Friday.
content.error_sentence: It was the manager which approved the new schedule last Friday.
content.corrected_sentence: It was the manager who approved the new schedule last Friday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: What she really wants are a longer holiday, not a pay rise.
content.error_sentence: What she really wants are a longer holiday, not a pay rise.
content.corrected_sentence: What she really wants is a longer holiday, not a pay rise.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: It was Anna found the missing keys under the sofa cushion yesterday.
content.error_sentence: It was Anna found the missing keys under the sofa cushion yesterday.
content.corrected_sentence: It was Anna who found the missing keys under the sofa cushion yesterday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Never I have seen such a well-organised event in this city before.
content.error_sentence: Never I have seen such a well-organised event in this city before.
content.corrected_sentence: Never have I seen such a well-organised event in this city before.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: What the team needs are more time to prepare for the final presentation.
content.error_sentence: What the team needs are more time to prepare for the final presentation.
content.corrected_sentence: What the team needs is more time to prepare for the final presentation.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: It was the broken cable which caused the power cut in the whole building.
content.error_sentence: It was the broken cable which caused the power cut in the whole building.
content.corrected_sentence: It was the broken cable that caused the power cut in the whole building.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Rarely she has spoken so confidently in front of such a large audience.
content.error_sentence: Rarely she has spoken so confidently in front of such a large audience.
content.corrected_sentence: Rarely has she spoken so confidently in front of such a large audience.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: It was the noise from the street which woke the baby up at three in the morning, not the alarm.
content.error_sentence: It was the noise from the street which woke the baby up at three in the morning, not the alarm.
content.corrected_sentence: It was the noise from the street that woke the baby up at three in the morning, not the alarm.
